title: GP Modeling
text:
This report picks up where nac.qmd’s Analysis Region / OHC Anomaly sections leave off, without re-running any of that report’s plotting or re-deriving its climatology. nac.qmd publishes the ANALYSIS_BOUNDS-restricted, multi-year truth field and the truth-vs-climatology anomaly cells to data/ohc_truth/; loading those directly (~1s) skips both the expensive depth-integration step (cached separately, per year, as data/ohc_truth/truth_ohc_<year>.nc) and the RG climatology reprocessing, neither of which this report needs to repeat.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]
OHC_TRUTH_DIR   = "../data/ohc_truth"
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]




Load nac.qmd’s published truth field and truth-vs-climatology anomaly
truth_field_all = xr.open_dataset(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc").load()
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.read_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", parse_dates=["month"])

title: GP Modeling
text:
Background
We interpolate real Argo float OHC (ohc_700/ohc_2000 directly, not their anomaly relative to climatology) onto the full analysis-domain grid at the native 1/12\(^\circ\) resolution, using a Gaussian process fit with GpGp. Each real float profile is assigned the GLORYS truth OHC at its position and date.
code/ohc_gp_fit.R fits one GP per depth (0-700m, 0-2000m) with the stationary spatiotemporal covariance function matern_spheretime, pooling all profiles across all three years, with covariance parameters \(\theta\) = (\(\sigma^2\), \(\rho_s\), \(\rho_t\), \(\nu\), \(\tau^2\)) = (variance, spatial range, temporal range, smoothness, nugget). Smoothness is fixed at \(\nu = \tfrac{1}{2}\), where the Matérn form reduces to the simple exponential \(k(r) = e^{-r}\).
The temporal coordinate is a continuous day count (days since 2020-01-01), not day-of-year – so the fitted temporal range can extend across years rather than being capped at within-year scale by an annual wraparound, letting the model express long-range temporal autocorrelation if the data support it.
Fitting and prediction are separate scripts, so one fit is reused across both prediction grids below. code/ohc_gp_predict.R predicts via a custom Vecchia kriging step (kriging_predict) that gives both a mean and a standard error – GpGp::predictions() alone is faster but has no SE. Predicting on the coarse 1° grid (35 points) conditions each point on every one of the ~3,450 pooled profiles (“exact” mode) – cheap at that grid size, and matches the reference. Predicting on the native 1/12° grid (~3,600 points x 36 months x 2 depths) with that same full conditioning does not scale – so that pass instead uses a bounded Vecchia neighbourhood (m = 30, matching the fit’s own approximation), which reuses the same underlying machinery but conditions each point on only its 30 nearest profiles.

title: GP Modeling
text:
Model Fit


Sample GLORYS truth OHC at each real Argo profile’s position/date
_dates = xr.DataArray(pd.to_datetime(argo_a["date"]).values, dims="profile")
_lats  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lat"].values, dims="profile")
_lons  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lon"].values, dims="profile")
_truth_at_real = truth_field_all.interp(time=_dates, latitude=_lats, longitude=_lons, method="nearest")

real_argo_direct = argo_a[["lat", "lon", "date"]].copy()
for _d in DEPTHS:
    real_argo_direct[_d] = _truth_at_real[_d].values




Fit the pooled GP once, then predict on the 1\(^\circ\) (exact) and native 1/12\(^\circ\) (bounded Vecchia) grids
_GP_DIR = "../data/gp_interp"
os.makedirs(_GP_DIR, exist_ok=True)
_real_profiles_csv    = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_profiles_real_direct.csv"
_real_fit_summary_csv = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_fit_summary.csv"
_real_model_cache     = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_model_cache.rds"

_keys = ["month", "cell_lat", "cell_lon"]
_GP_MONTH_DAY = "middle"
_GP_RES_LABELS = {1 / 12: "1/12$^\circ$ (native)", 1.0: "1$^\circ$"}
# Full conditioning ("exact") is fine at 35 points; the ~3,600-point native
# grid needs the bounded Vecchia approximation to stay fast -- see Background.
_GP_PREDICT_M = {1 / 12: 30, 1.0: "exact"}
_pred_grid_csvs = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_pred_grid_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}
_real_out_csvs  = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_predictions_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}

def _grid_lats_lons(deg):
    """Prediction grid for one resolution -- native truth grid, or a deg-degree coarsening of it."""
    if deg < 0.1:
        return truth_field_all["latitude"].values, truth_field_all["longitude"].values
    _tc = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
    return np.sort(_tc["cell_lat"].unique()), np.sort(_tc["cell_lon"].unique())

for deg, grid_csv in _pred_grid_csvs.items():
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    ohc_clim.build_pred_grid(lats, lons).to_csv(grid_csv, index=False)

ohc_clim.write_profile_csv(real_argo_direct, _real_profiles_csv, suffix="")
# Fit + predict are the expensive steps (pooled GP fit, bounded-Vecchia predict
# on the ~3,600-point native grid); skip both if the fit summary and model
# cache are already on disk. Delete both files to force a re-fit/re-predict.
if not (os.path.exists(_real_fit_summary_csv) and os.path.exists(_real_model_cache)):
    ohc_clim.run_gp_fit(_real_profiles_csv, _real_fit_summary_csv, _real_model_cache)
    for deg, out_csv in _real_out_csvs.items():
        ohc_clim.run_gp_predict(
            _real_profiles_csv, _pred_grid_csvs[deg], _real_model_cache, out_csv,
            m=_GP_PREDICT_M[deg], month_day=_GP_MONTH_DAY,
        )

gp_fit_summary = ohc_clim.load_gp_fit_summary(_real_fit_summary_csv)
gp_fit_summary.groupby("depth")[["loglik", "converged", "n_obs"]].first()

Load the GP-predicted OHC field directly (no climatology to add back)
def build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df=None):
    """Load GP predictions from out_csv and assemble the display context for resolution deg.

    Modeling OHC directly (suffix="" throughout) means the GP's prediction *is*
    the field -- no per-year climatology reassembly needed here, unlike the
    anomaly-modeling case.
    """
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    gp_field = ohc_clim.load_gp_anomaly_field(out_csv, lats, lons, suffix="")

    cells    = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field)
    se_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field, value_cols=("ohc_700_se", "ohc_2000_se"))
    obs_cells = (
        ohc.grid_cells(profiles_df, list(DEPTHS), deg=1 / 12)
        if profiles_df is not None else None
    )

    if deg < 0.1:
        truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
        _bf = (gp_field[list(DEPTHS)] - truth_monthly_all[list(DEPTHS)]).rename(
            {d: f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS}
        )
        bias_cells  = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(_bf, value_cols=[f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS])
        truth_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)
    else:
        # coarsen_truth resamples+bins by each row's own date, so one call over
        # all three concatenated years already yields distinct per-year cells.
        truth_by_deg_all = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
        _merged = cells.merge(
            truth_by_deg_all[_keys + list(DEPTHS)].rename(columns={d: f"{d}_truth" for d in DEPTHS}),
            on=_keys, how="left",
        )
        bias_cells = _merged.copy()
        for _d in DEPTHS:
            bias_cells[f"{_d}_bias"] = bias_cells[_d] - bias_cells[f"{_d}_truth"]
        truth_cells = truth_by_deg_all

    return dict(
        cells=cells, se_cells=se_cells, obs_cells=obs_cells,
        bias_cells=bias_cells, truth_cells=truth_cells,
        obs_profiles=profiles_df,
    )

def build_gp_ctx(out_csvs, profiles_df=None):
    return {_GP_RES_LABELS[deg]: build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df) for deg, out_csv in out_csvs.items()}

_real_ctx = build_gp_ctx(_real_out_csvs, profiles_df=real_argo_direct)




Format the pooled GP fit parameter table for display
def _fmt_fit_summary(df):
    d = df.copy()
    d["display"] = d.apply(
        lambda r: f"{r['estimate']:.4g}" if pd.isna(r["std_error"])
        else f"{r['estimate']:.4g} +/- {r['std_error']:.3g}",
        axis=1,
    )
    wide = d.pivot(index="parameter", columns="depth", values="display")
    order = ["mean (intercept)", "variance", "spatial_range", "temporal_range", "smoothness", "nugget"]
    return wide.reindex([p for p in order if p in wide.index])

EARTH_RADIUS_KM = 6371.0
_scale = {
    "mean (intercept)": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "variance": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ ** 2,
    "spatial_range": EARTH_RADIUS_KM, "temporal_range": 1.0,
    "smoothness": 1.0, "nugget": 1.0,
}
_disp = gp_fit_summary.copy()
_disp["estimate"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["estimate"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
_disp["std_error"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["std_error"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
fit_table = _fmt_fit_summary(_disp)
fit_table.index.name = "parameter"
fit_table

title: Outlook
text:
Trajectory Simulation
The bounds above are analytic and design-based. As an independent, physically-grounded check we will (in a later phase) generate an ensemble of float samplings under the real GLORYS advection using VirtualFleet, mapping each realization through the same run_simulation \(\to\) float_ohc pipeline and computing its realized bias against the proxy as in Validation (realized preferential-sampling bias \(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i Y(x_i) - \mu\); total bias the GP space-time mean minus truth). The simulator is currently deterministic (one realization per configuration), so an ensemble requires injecting controlled randomness – deployment jitter, and optionally a stochastic turbulent-diffusion kernel – to obtain independent members. The empirical distribution of the physically-realized bias, wrapped in a nonparametric one-sided tolerance bound, then serves as a reality check that the analytic caps derived here actually cover the bias produced by floats drifting in a genuine western-boundary-current field.

title: Prediction Bias Bounds
text:
For the interpolate-everywhere estimator the total bias \(B = \hat\mu - \mu\) is itself a linear functional of the field, and can be bounded using the structure of the interpolator (this subsumes the prediction term \(B_{\mathrm{pred}}\), since the sampling geometry is carried by the weights). OHC is markedly non-stationary in this western-boundary-current region – its variance and correlation ranges vary sharply across the NAC front (cf. Baugh and McKinnon 2022) – so we fit a non-stationary Gaussian-process covariance \(k(x,x')\) on the sphere-plus-time domain and take its reproducing-kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) \(\mathcal{H}_k\) as the smoothness class for the field (Stein 1999; Muandet et al. 2017). Nothing in the derivation below assumes stationarity: the reproducing property, the Riesz representer, the closed-form \(\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\), and the posterior-SE identity all hold verbatim for a general symmetric positive-definite \(k\).
The resulting bound is not automatic: it holds only under a set of assumptions, which we state rather than assume away.

Correctly specified kernel. The bound is only as trustworthy as \(\mathcal{H}_k\)’s description of the field, and this is the binding assumption. A non-stationary \(k\) is what makes it plausible: it can match OHC’s spatially varying variance and range across the front, whereas a stationary kernel cannot. It remains an assumption – \(k\) must be well specified, with \(Y \in \mathcal{H}_k\) – that we flag rather than treat as settled.
Target in the RKHS, with known scale. \(Y \in \mathcal{H}_k\) and \(\|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R\); the radius \(R\) is estimated below, so the computed cap is itself an estimate.
Noiseless point evaluations at the profiles (an observation-error variance \(\tau^2\) is accommodated by the final callout of this section).
Fill distance / conditioning of the observation set governs the size of \(\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\): sparse or clustered space-time sampling inflates it, as it should.

title: Prediction Bias Bounds
text:
Linearity and the worst-case (minimax) bound
Both the interpolated estimator and the target are linear functionals of the field. The kriging predictor of the space-time mean is
\[\begin{gather*}
\hat\mu \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i\, Y(x_i),
\end{gather*}\]
a fixed linear combination of the \(n\) observations at \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) with kriging weights \(\omega_i\); the target is \(L(Y) = \mu = \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y\, d\nu\). Their difference is the residual functional
\[\begin{gather*}
\Delta(Y) \;=\; L(Y) - \sum_i \omega_i\, Y(x_i).
\end{gather*}\]
Over an RKHS ball \(\{\, Y : \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R \,\}\), the worst-case reconstruction bias has a closed form (the minimax linear-estimation duality of Donoho (1994); the representer theorem, Wahba (1990)):
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R} \big| \Delta(Y) \big| \;=\; R\, \big\| \eta \big\|_{\mathcal{H}}, \qquad
\eta \;=\; \xi_\nu - \sum_i \omega_i\, k(\cdot, x_i), \qquad
\xi_\nu(\cdot) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(\cdot, x)\, d\nu(x) , \;}
\end{gather*}\]
where \(\xi_\nu \in \mathcal{H}\) is the Riesz representer of the space-time-mean functional (\(\langle \xi_\nu, f\rangle_{\mathcal{H}} = L(f)\) for all \(f\)); the defining integral is a Bochner integral in \(\mathcal{H}\), well defined since \(k\) is bounded and \(\nu\) finite.






NoteDerivation: the residual functional is \(\langle Y, \eta\rangle\), and its worst case is \(R\|\eta\|\)





The one fact that makes everything linear-in-\(Y\) collapse is the reproducing property: for every \(f \in \mathcal{H}_k\) and location \(x\), \(f(x) = \langle f, k(\cdot, x)\rangle_{\mathcal{H}}\) (Muandet et al. 2017). Hence each point evaluation is an inner product, and so is the space-time-mean functional, by exchanging the (Bochner) integral with the inner product – valid since \(x \mapsto k(\cdot,x)\) is Bochner-\(\nu\)-integrable (\(k\) bounded, \(\nu\) finite):
\[\begin{align*}
L(Y) &= \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y(x)\, d\nu(x)
= \int_{\mathcal{X}} \langle Y, k(\cdot,x)\rangle\, d\nu(x) \\
&= \Big\langle Y,\ \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(\cdot,x)\, d\nu(x) \Big\rangle
= \langle Y, \xi_\nu\rangle .
\end{align*}\]
This identifies the Riesz representer \(\xi_\nu(\cdot) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(\cdot,x)\,
d\nu(x)\). Writing the point evaluations the same way, the residual functional becomes a single inner product:
\[\begin{align*}
\Delta(Y) &= \langle Y, \xi_\nu\rangle - \sum_i \omega_i \langle Y, k(\cdot,x_i)\rangle \\
&= \Big\langle Y,\ \xi_\nu - \sum_i \omega_i\, k(\cdot,x_i)\Big\rangle
= \langle Y, \eta\rangle .
\end{align*}\]
Maximizing over the ball is then just Cauchy–Schwarz in \(\mathcal{H}\):
\[\begin{gather*}
\sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R} \big|\langle Y, \eta\rangle\big| = R\,\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}},
\end{gather*}\]
attained at the worst-case field \(Y^\star = R\,\eta/\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\) – the smoothest field of norm \(R\) that the observations cannot pin down.



The norm of the residual representer is computable in closed form from the kernel alone:
\[\begin{gather*}
\big\| \eta \big\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2 = \underbrace{\iint k(x,x')\,d\nu(x)\,d\nu(x')}_{\langle \xi_\nu,\xi_\nu\rangle}
\;-\; 2\sum_i \omega_i \int k(x, x_i)\, d\nu(x) \;+\; \sum_{i,j} \omega_i\, \omega_j\, k(x_i, x_j).
\end{gather*}\]






NoteDerivation: the closed-form representer norm





Expand \(\|\eta\|^2 = \langle \eta, \eta\rangle\) with \(\eta = \xi_\nu - \sum_i \omega_i k(\cdot, x_i)\) bilinearly:
\[\begin{gather*}
\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2 = \langle \xi_\nu, \xi_\nu\rangle - 2\sum_i \omega_i\, \langle \xi_\nu, k(\cdot, x_i)\rangle
+ \sum_{i,j}\omega_i\,\omega_j\, \langle k(\cdot, x_i), k(\cdot, x_j)\rangle .
\end{gather*}\]
Each inner product is evaluated by the reproducing property, \(\langle g, k(\cdot, x)\rangle = g(x)\):

\(\langle k(\cdot, x_i), k(\cdot, x_j)\rangle = k(x_i, x_j)\) – the Gram matrix of the observations;
\(\langle \xi_\nu, k(\cdot, x_i)\rangle = \xi_\nu(x_i) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(x, x_i)\,
d\nu(x)\) – the mean-to-obs cross terms;
\(\langle \xi_\nu, \xi_\nu\rangle = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \xi_\nu(x)\, d\nu(x)
= \iint k(x,x')\,d\nu(x)\,d\nu(x')\).

Substituting gives the boxed formula. Nothing but the kernel \(k\), the space-time measure \(\nu\), and the kriging weights \(\omega\) enters – no knowledge of \(Y\) is required to evaluate the bound (though \(k\) must be the correctly specified kernel, per the assumptions above).



This is exactly the kriging (posterior) variance of the space-time-mean functional, and since \(\Delta(Y) = L(Y) - \sum_i \omega_i Y(x_i) = \mu - \hat\mu\), the bound caps the estimator’s total bias \(|B|\) (under the assumptions above). The radius \(R\) sets the assumed smoothness scale of the field (\(\|Y\|_{\mathcal H} \le R\)); it is estimated from the data via the fitted process variance \(\sigma^2\) (and cross-checked against the reanalysis in validation), so the deterministic cap \(|B| \le R\, \|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\) is itself an estimate rather than a quantity known a priori.

title: Prediction Bias Bounds
section: Link to the posterior standard error already computed
text:
Link to the posterior standard error already computed
When the weights \(\omega\) are the kriging-optimal ones – exactly those our GP predictor uses – the residual-representer norm equals the GP posterior standard deviation of the space-time mean, \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\). Hence the deterministic minimax cap is a known multiple of the posterior SE,
\[\begin{gather*}
\big| B \big| \;\le\; R\,\|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}} \;=\; R\cdot \mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}, \qquad R = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_k} \;=\; \rho/\sigma,
\end{gather*}\]
where \(\rho = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_r}\) is the reference field’s norm in the unit-variance correlation kernel and \(\sigma\) the fitted process standard deviation. That posterior SE is precisely what ohc_gp_predict.R::kriging_predict already returns and what nac_gp.qmd already turns into \(\pm 1\) SE and \(\pm 1.96\) SE prediction intervals. The existing \(\pm 1.96\,\mathrm{SE}\) coverage-rate calibration against the reference field is therefore precisely the honesty check for the probabilistic version of this bound: if the nominal intervals cover it at their stated rate, the posterior SE is a trustworthy (probabilistic) bound on \(B\); the minimax result upgrades it to a deterministic one at the cost of the single dimensionless constant \(R\).
The \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\) identity and this coverage check are kernel-agnostic and hold for the non-stationary \(k\) above. The specific figures currently produced by nac_gp.qmd come from its exploratory stationary matern_spheretime fit and are shown as illustrative, pending the non-stationary implementation; the identities carry over unchanged once \(k\) is replaced.






NoteDerivation: why \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\)





Write the observation Gram matrix \(K_{ij} = k(x_i, x_j)\) and the mean-to-obs vector \(c_i = \xi_\nu(x_i) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(x,x_i)\,d\nu(x)\). The (noiseless) simple-kriging weights minimize \(\|\eta\|^2\) over \(\omega\); setting the gradient of the closed-form norm to zero,
\[\begin{gather*}
\nabla_\omega\Big(\langle\xi_\nu,\xi_\nu\rangle - 2\,\omega^\top c + \omega^\top K \omega\Big) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \omega^\star = K^{-1} c .
\end{gather*}\]
Substituting back,
\[\begin{align*}
\|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2
&= \langle\xi_\nu,\xi_\nu\rangle - 2\,\omega^{\star\top} c + \omega^{\star\top} K \omega^\star \\
&= \langle\xi_\nu,\xi_\nu\rangle - c^\top K^{-1} c .
\end{align*}\]
This is identically the Gaussian-process posterior variance of the linear functional \(L(Y)\) given the observations,
\[\begin{gather*}
\operatorname{Var}\!\big(L(Y) \mid Y(x_1),\dots,Y(x_n)\big) = \underbrace{\textstyle\iint k\,d\nu\,d\nu}_{\langle\xi_\nu,\xi_\nu\rangle} - c^\top K^{-1} c ,
\end{gather*}\]
so \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\). (A nugget/observation-error variance \(\tau^2\) simply replaces \(K\) by \(K + \tau^2 I\) throughout, adding the usual measurement-noise term to the posterior variance; the identity is otherwise unchanged.)

title: Prediction Bias Bounds
section: Computing the bound: what must be estimated
text:
Computing the bound: what must be estimated
Stripped to essentials, the minimax bound is \(|B| \le R\,\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\): the posterior standard error of the space-time-mean – a number the GP pipeline already produces – times a single smoothness radius \(R\). Everything else is deterministic kernel bookkeeping and needs no knowledge of the field \(Y\). Three objects carry the meaning:

\(\xi_\nu\), the Riesz representer of the mean functional: the (unique) element of \(\mathcal{H}_k\) whose inner product with any field returns that field’s space-time average. It is computed once from the kernel, \(\xi_\nu(\cdot) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(\cdot,x)\,d\nu(x)\).
\(\eta = \xi_\nu - \sum_i \omega_i\,k(\cdot,x_i)\), the residual representer: what the estimator, a weighted sum of point evaluations, fails to capture of \(\xi_\nu\). Its norm \(\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\) is the worst-case leverage the unknown part of the field has on the bias, and at the kriging-optimal weights it equals \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\).
\(R = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_k}\), the smoothness radius: how “large” (rough/variable) the true field is allowed to be in the kernel’s geometry. It is the one input beyond the kernel, and the only genuinely field-dependent quantity in the bound.

The high-level recipe (deliberately omitting numerical specifics like quadrature rules) has inputs the profile space-time locations \(x_1,\dots,x_n\) and the domain \(\mathcal{X}\) with its measure \(\nu\) – no field values \(Y\) are needed for the bound itself – and proceeds in five steps:

Fit the covariance kernel \(k\). Fit the (non-stationary) GP covariance to the profiles: estimate the hyperparameters – process variance \(\sigma^2\), spatial and temporal length-scales, smoothness, nugget \(\tau^2\), and any non-stationarity parameters. This fixes \(k\) and hence the smoothness class \(\mathcal{H}_k\).
Assemble three kernel quantities (deterministic given \(k\); no \(Y\)):

the Gram matrix \(K_{ij} = k(x_i, x_j)\), of size \(n\times n\);
the mean-to-obs vector \(c_i = \int_{\mathcal{X}} k(x, x_i)\,d\nu(x)\), of length \(n\) (by quadrature);
the mean-to-mean scalar \(v = \iint k(x, x')\,d\nu(x)\,d\nu(x')\) (by quadrature).

Posterior SE of the space-time mean (this equals \(\|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\)). Solve the kriging system \(\omega^\star = (K + \tau^2 I)^{-1} c\) and form

Equivalently, read \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\) straight from the GP predictor’s output for the space-time-mean functional – it already computes this.

Choose the smoothness radius \(R = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_k}\). Write \(k = \sigma^2\, r\) with \(r\) the unit-variance correlation kernel; then \(R = \rho/\sigma\), where \(\rho\) is the dimensionless RKHS norm of the standardized field. Calibrate \(R\) (equivalently \(\rho\)) from the data or the reanalysis so the implied worst-case field is physically plausible. This is the assumption-laden step; steps 1–3 are not.
The minimax bound: \(\;|B| \le R\,\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\).

Reading the recipe back: steps 1–3 turn the kernel and the sampling geometry into \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\), entirely field-free; step 4 supplies the single smoothness scale \(R\) that converts “how much the observations fail to pin down the mean” into “how far the bias can actually be”; step 5 multiplies them. Sparse or clustered profiles enlarge \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}}\) (and thus the bound), as they should, and a rougher assumed field (larger \(R\)) enlarges it further.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
Preferential-Sampling Bounds
Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
\(L^2\) Worst-Case Bound
Assume
\[\begin{gather*}
Y,\;w-1\in L^2(\nu),
\end{gather*}\]
and write \(\sigma_Y = \|Y-\mu\|_2\) for the space-time standard deviation of the field and \(\sigma_w =
\|w-1\|_2\) for the coefficient of variation of the sampling intensity – a resolution-free measure of how inhomogeneous the sampling is, finite precisely because \(w \in L^2(\nu)\) (itself a genuine restriction: it excludes highly concentrated designs, and in the limit the purely atomic empirical pattern of a finite sample, which is not absolutely continuous w.r.t. \(\nu\); see Bias decomposition). Applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in \(L^2(\nu)\) to the covariance gives a bound that holds for any field and any design:
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; |B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \;=\; |\operatorname{Cov}(w,Y)| \;=\; |\rho_{w,Y}|\,\sigma_w\,\sigma_Y \;\le\; \sigma_w\,\sigma_Y . \;}
\end{gather*}\]
This bound requires only finite second moments and imposes no smoothness assumptions on the underlying field; \(\rho_{w,Y} = 1\) is the worst case.
Neither \(\sigma_Y\) nor \(\sigma_w\) is directly observable: \(\sigma_w\) requires an estimated intensity \(\hat w\) from the float density, and \(\sigma_Y\) requires a field estimate (\(\hat\sigma_Y\) from the GP, Prediction Bias Bounds) or the reanalysis proxy used in validation (Validation). The inequality is field-free; the computed cap is an estimate.






NoteDiagnostic: can \(\rho_{w,Y}\) itself be estimated, to beat the worst case \(\rho_{w,Y}=1\)?





In principle: a plug-in Pearson estimate \(\hat\rho_{w,Y} = \widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(\hat w,\hat
Y)/(\hat\sigma_w\hat\sigma_Y)\), formed by evaluating \(\hat w\) and \(\hat Y\) (or the GLORYS proxy) at a common set of quadrature points, would replace the worst case with an empirical value typically well below 1. But \(\sigma_w\) and \(\sigma_Y\) are marginal quantities – each estimable from one side of the problem alone (float density; field) – whereas \(\rho_{w,Y}\) is exactly the joint dependence between sampling and field, i.e. the preferential-sampling signal itself. Plugging in \(\hat\rho_{w,Y}\) does not tighten a distribution-free worst-case bound; it is algebraically the same computation as estimating \(B_{\mathrm{samp}} = \operatorname{Cov}(w,Y)\) directly. The report already carries that quantity, but as the proxy-evaluated realized bias (Validation), checked against the permutation null below, not reported as a deterministic cap. A \(\hat\rho_{w,Y}\) diagnostic is worth computing alongside the permutation test – how far \(|\hat\rho_{w,Y}|\) falls below 1 in practice – but it estimates the bias itself rather than certifying a bound on it.




Illustrative Example
To read off a scale for the cap, consider one particular idealized intensity: floats cover a space-time sub-region \(D_f \subseteq \mathcal{X}\) of measure \(f = \nu(D_f)\) uniformly and sample nowhere else, so \(\pi(E) = \nu(E \cap D_f)/f\), whose density is \(w = 1/f\) on \(D_f\) and \(w = 0\) off it. Then \(\sigma_w^2 = (1-f)/f\) and
\[\begin{gather*}
\big| B_{\mathrm{samp}} \big| \;\le\; \sigma_Y \sqrt{\tfrac{1-f}{f}} .
\end{gather*}\]
The factor \(\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\) is a property of this hard-footprint intensity, not of preferential sampling in general; the operative cap remains \(\sigma_w\,\sigma_Y\) for whatever intensity holds.






ImportantHeuristic: what the covered fraction \(f\) requires





This closed form is an illustration, not the operative bound. The empirical measure \(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \delta_{x_i}\) of a finite point pattern is purely atomic, hence not absolutely continuous with respect to \(\nu\) and admitting no density \(w \in L^2(\nu)\); the covered fraction \(f = \nu(\bigcup_i
\text{footprint}x_i)\) is defined only after each float is assigned a representativeness footprint (e.g. a disc of one correlation length in space and time) that smooths the atoms into a design \(\pi \ll \nu\). Unlike the general cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\), a functional of the intensity alone, the closed form inherits a dependence on that footprint scale, and shrinking the scale drives \(f \to 0\) and the cap \(\to \infty\). We therefore report \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) as the operative cap and \(\sigma_Y\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\) only for interpretation.









NoteDerivation: the closed form for \(\sigma_w\)





Take the hard-footprint density \(w = 1/f\) on \(D_f\) (with \(\nu(D_f) = f\)) and \(w = 0\) on the complement (measure \(1-f\)). Then
\[\begin{align*}
\sigma_w^2 &= \int_{\mathcal{X}} (w - 1)^2\,d\nu \\
&= f\Big(\tfrac{1}{f}-1\Big)^2 + (1-f)(0-1)^2 \\
&= \frac{(1-f)^2}{f} + (1-f) = (1-f)\Big(\frac{1-f}{f} + 1\Big) = \frac{1-f}{f}.
\end{align*}\]
Hence \(|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \le \sigma_Y\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\); any other intensity only changes \(\sigma_w\), with the general cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) still holding exactly.



The amplification factor \(\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\) diverges as coverage \(\to 0\) and vanishes as coverage \(\to 1\):


Illustrative: the amplification factor
f = np.linspace(0.01, 1.0, 400)
amp = np.sqrt((1 - f) / f)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.plot(f, amp, color="#1f77b4", lw=2)
ax.set_xlabel("covered space-time fraction  f")
ax.set_ylabel(r"amplification  $\sqrt{(1-f)/f}$")
ax.set_ylim(0, 6)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
for fv in (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5):
    ax.annotate(f"f={fv:g}: x{np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv):.1f}",
                xy=(fv, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv)),
                xytext=(fv + 0.03, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv) + 0.3),
                fontsize=8, color="#444")
    ax.plot(fv, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv), "o", color="#d62728", ms=4)
_show_fig(fig)








Figure 1: Illustrative amplification factor sqrt((1-f)/f) vs the covered space-time fraction f, under one particular (hard-footprint) intensity. In that special case the cap on |B^samp| equals this factor times sigma_Y, the standard deviation of the true OHC field over space-time; the operative bound is the general sigma_w * sigma_Y for whatever intensity holds, and f depends on an assumed representativeness footprint (see note). Sparse coverage (small f) inflates the worst-case bias sharply.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
RKHS Worst-Case Bound
The Cauchy–Schwarz cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) is distribution-free: it assumes nothing about the field beyond a finite variance. Suppose instead the field is smooth, such that it lives in the RKHS \(\mathcal{H}_k\) of a covariance kernel \(k\) – the same assumption already made for the total bias in Prediction Bias Bounds.
Beyond the uniform space-time target measure \(\nu\) already fixed in Motivation and estimand, consider the realized sampling measure \(\pi\), \(d\pi = w\,d\nu\) (so \(\pi(\mathcal X) = \int_{\mathcal X} w\,d\nu =
1\): a genuine probability measure, the distribution the floats actually realize).
Conditional on the design (so \(w\), and hence \(\pi\), is fixed), the sampling bias is a bounded linear functional of the field – linear in \(Y\) because \(w\) does not depend on it,
\[\begin{gather*}
B_{\mathrm{samp}} \;=\; L(Y) \;=\; \int_{\mathcal X} Y(x)\bigl(w(x)-1\bigr)\,d\nu(x),
\end{gather*}\]
which, since \(d\pi = w\,d\nu\), splits as
\[\begin{gather*}
L(Y) \;=\; \int_{\mathcal X} Y(x)\,d\pi(x) - \int_{\mathcal X} Y(x)\,d\nu(x) .
\end{gather*}\]
Applying the reproducing property \(Y(x) = \langle Y,k(\cdot,x)\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}\) (Muandet et al. 2017) inside each integral and pulling \(Y\) out of the (Bochner) integral,
\[\begin{align*}
L(Y) &= \int \left\langle Y,\, k(\cdot,x) \right\rangle \,d\pi(x) - \int \left\langle Y,\, k(\cdot,x) \right\rangle \,d\nu(x) \\
&= \left\langle Y,\ \int k(\cdot,x)\,d\pi(x)\right\rangle - \left\langle Y,\ \int k(\cdot,x)\,d\nu(x)\right\rangle \\
&= \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\pi} \right\rangle - \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\nu} \right\rangle \;=\; \left\langle Y,\, \xi_{\pi} - \xi_{\nu} \right\rangle ,
\end{align*}\]
where \(\xi_\pi := \int_{\mathcal X} k(\cdot,x)\,d\pi(x)\) and \(\xi_\nu := \int_{\mathcal X}
k(\cdot,x)\,d\nu(x)\) are the kernel mean embeddings of \(\pi\) and \(\nu\) into \(\mathcal H_k\) (Smola et al. 2007; Muandet et al. 2017) – the same \(\xi_\nu\) that reappears as the Riesz representer of the mean functional in Prediction Bias Bounds. Writing \(\psi := \xi_\pi - \xi_\nu\), this is exactly \(L(Y) = \langle
Y,\psi\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}\): by the Riesz Representation Theorem, \(\psi\) is the unique element of \(\mathcal H_k\) representing \(L\), and we have derived it in closed form,
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \psi \;=\; \xi_\pi - \xi_\nu \;=\; \int_{\mathcal X} k(\cdot,x)\bigl(w(x)-1\bigr)\,d\nu(x). \;}
\end{gather*}\]
This gives \(\psi\) a direct geometric reading: it is the discrepancy, in the RKHS, between where the floats actually sampled and the uniform target measure – exactly zero when \(\pi=\nu\) (non-preferential sampling) – and its norm \(\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k} = \|\xi_\pi - \xi_\nu\|_{\mathcal H_k}\) is precisely the (kernel) maximum mean discrepancy between \(\pi\) and \(\nu\) (Gretton et al. 2012), so the RKHS worst-case cap below is \(R\) times an MMD between the realized sampling distribution and the target.
Applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality in the RKHS,
\[\begin{gather*}
|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| = |\langle Y,\psi\rangle_{\mathcal H_k}| \le \|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\,\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k},
\end{gather*}\]
so if \(\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\le R\), maximizing over the smoothness ball gives – the optimal-recovery / minimax-linear-functional duality (Micchelli and Rivlin 1977; Donoho 1994):
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k} \le R} |B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \;=\; R\,\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}, \;}
\end{gather*}\]
attained at the worst-case field \(Y^\star = R\psi/\|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}\). Compared with the \(L^2\) bound, the RKHS bound exploits the smoothness encoded by the kernel; since the admissible class of functions is substantially smaller than all of \(L^2(\nu)\), this bound is typically much tighter.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
section: Computing the RKHS Norm of the Representer
text:
Computing the RKHS Norm of the Representer
The RKHS norm of the representer admits the closed-form expression
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \|\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}^2 = \iint_{\mathcal X\times\mathcal X} k(x,y)\bigl(w(x)-1\bigr)\bigl(w(y)-1\bigr)\,d\nu(x)\,d\nu(y). \;}
\end{gather*}\]
Importantly, this quantity depends only on the sampling design and the chosen covariance kernel, not on the unknown OHC field – in practice, the same non-stationary kernel fit in Prediction Bias Bounds.
Since the sampling density \(w\) is unknown, it must first be estimated from the observed float locations. Possible approaches include

kernel density estimation (KDE),
Gaussian process intensity models,
log-Gaussian Cox processes, or
other smooth spatial or spatio-temporal point process models.

Once an estimate \(\hat w\) has been obtained, the double integral can be evaluated numerically using quadrature or Monte Carlo integration. If \(x_1,\ldots,x_m\) are grid points sampled approximately uniformly with respect to \(\nu\), then
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \|\hat\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}^2 \approx \frac1{m^2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} k(x_i,x_j)(\hat w_i-1)(\hat w_j-1). \;}
\end{gather*}\]

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
Estimating the RKHS Radius
The remaining quantity,
\[\begin{gather*}
R=\|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k},
\end{gather*}\]
measures the complexity of the unknown true field relative to the chosen kernel.
Possible approaches include
1. Plug-in estimate from the fitted Gaussian process. Let \(\hat Y\) denote the posterior mean from a fitted Gaussian process model. The RKHS norm \(\|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}\) provides a natural measure of the complexity of the reconstructed field. Because GP regression regularizes the estimate, this quantity should be viewed as a proxy (or approximate lower bound) for the unknown RKHS norm of the true field. For noiseless kernel interpolation,
\[\begin{gather*}
\hat Y(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i k(x_i,x), \qquad \alpha=K^{-1}y,
\end{gather*}\]
and
\[\begin{gather*}
\boxed{\; \|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}^2 = y^\top K^{-1}y. \;}
\end{gather*}\]
Where \(K_{ij} = k(x_i,x_j)\), the GP covariance matrix.
Since this interpolant is the minimum-RKHS-norm function matching the data,
\[\begin{gather*}
\|\hat Y\|_{\mathcal H_k} \le \|Y\|_{\mathcal H_k}.
\end{gather*}\]
In Gaussian process regression (kriging), the posterior mean generally does not exactly interpolate the observations because of measurement error or nugget effects. Consequently, the quadratic form above is replaced by the corresponding expression involving the covariance matrix and observation error covariance (e.g., \((K+\Sigma_\varepsilon)^{-1}\)). The interpretation remains the same: the fitted GP norm serves as a regularized measure of field complexity.
2. Sensitivity analysis. Since \(R\) cannot be identified from the observations alone, an alternative is to report
\[\begin{gather*}
|B_{\mathrm{samp}}| \le R\,\|\hat\psi\|_{\mathcal H_k}
\end{gather*}\]
over a range of plausible values of \(R\). This separates uncertainty due to the unknown smoothness of the field from uncertainty due to the sampling design.

title: Preferential-Sampling Bounds
text:
Summary
Both bounds are Cauchy–Schwarz applied to the same covariance \(\int (w-1)(Y-\mu)\,d\nu\), but in different inner-product spaces – and that changes which fields the worst case ranges over.

In \(L^2(\nu)\) (the bound \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\)): the worst-case field is \(Y-\mu \propto w-1\). Distribution-free, but pessimistic.
In \(\mathcal{H}_k\) (the bound \(R\|\psi\|\)): the worst-case field is \(Y^\star \propto \psi\).

title: Argo Bias
text: This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.

title: Argo Bias
text: This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.

title: Motivation, Estimand, and Bias Decomposition
text:
Motivation and Estimand
Argo floats sample the ocean sparsely and non-randomly. In a region with a strong current and a sharp heat-content gradient – our North Atlantic Current (NAC) box \(A = [36^\circ,40^\circ]\,\mathrm{N}
\times [-68^\circ,-62^\circ]\,\mathrm{E}\) – floats are advected along the flow and may preferentially occupy waters whose ocean heat content (OHC) differs systematically from the regional average. Any estimate of regional-mean OHC built by interpolating float observations therefore inherits a sampling-induced bias. This report states, explicitly, the mathematics behind the objects we use to characterize that bias. These are conceptually distinct and are kept separate throughout: (i) deterministic worst-case upper bounds – a design-based Cauchy–Schwarz cap (Preferential Sampling Bounds) and a model-based RKHS minimax cap (Prediction Bias Bounds); (ii) an illustrative approximation that interprets the design cap; and (iii) a permutation-based null distribution – a reference distribution, not a bound. Each is checked against a complete-coverage reanalysis (Validation).
Write \(Y(x)\) for the true OHC field over region \(A\) and study period \(\mathcal{T}\) (per depth horizon \(0\)–\(700\) m or \(0\)–\(2000\) m) – a continuous field on the sphere-plus-time domain \(\mathcal{X} := A \times \mathcal{T}\), with \(A \subset S^2\) the NAC box above and \(\mathcal{T} \subset \mathbb{R}\) the observation period. We write \(x = (s,t) \in \mathcal{X}\) for a generic space-time point – spatial location \(s \in A\), time \(t \in \mathcal{T}\) – and use \(x\) in place of \((s,t)\) throughout. Equip \(A\) with its Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{B}(A)\) (generated by the open subsets of \(A\)) and \(\mathcal{T}\) with \(\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T})\), giving the product measurable space \((\mathcal{X},\, \mathcal{B}(A)\otimes\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T}))\); every measure and integral below is taken on it. Let \(|\cdot|\) denote surface (area) measure on \((A, \mathcal{B}(A))\) and \(\alpha\) the induced normalized area probability measure, \(\alpha(E) = |E|/|A|\) for \(E \in \mathcal{B}(A)\), and let \(\tau\) be a normalized (probability) measure on \(\mathcal{T}\) (uniform over the study period unless stated otherwise). Write \(\nu := \alpha\otimes\tau\) for the resulting space-time probability measure on \(\mathcal{X}\); then \((\mathcal{X},\; \mathcal{B}(A)\otimes\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T}),\; \nu)\) is a probability space, and the estimand is the true space-time-mean OHC, the Lebesgue integral of \(Y\) against \(\nu\):
\[\begin{gather*}
\mu \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y \,d\nu \;=\; \mathbb{E}[Y] .
\end{gather*}\]
Notational convention. Throughout, unsubscripted expectations \(\mathbb{E}\), variances \(\operatorname{Var}\), covariances \(\operatorname{Cov}\), standard deviations (\(\sigma_Y\), \(\sigma_w\)), correlations (\(\rho_{w,Y}\)), and \(L^2\) inner products are all taken with respect to \(\nu\); we drop the subscript. Expectations over the sampling point process carry an explicit subscript \(\mathbb{E}_N\) to keep the two kinds of averaging distinct. (In lat/lon/time coordinates \(d\nu(s,t) = a(s)\,\theta(t)\,ds\,dt\), with \(a \propto \cos(\mathrm{lat})\) the Radon–Nikodym derivative of \(\alpha\) w.r.t. coordinate Lebesgue measure (\(\int_A a\,ds = 1\)) and \(\theta\) that of \(\tau\) (\(\int_{\mathcal{T}} \theta\,dt = 1\)); this is only the computational form.) A purely spatial, synoptic estimand is the special case \(\tau =
\delta_{t_0}\), giving \(\mu_{t_0} = \int_A Y(\cdot,t_0)\,d\alpha\); every identity and bound below specializes to it verbatim. We take the intensive (mean) OHC as primary because integrating against a probability measure is what makes \(\mathbb{E}\) an expectation, \(\sigma_Y\) a genuine standard deviation, and \(\rho_{w,Y}\) a correlation; the extensive total OHC is the fixed rescaling \(\mathrm{OHC}_{\mathrm{tot}} = |A|\,\mu\) by the known region area \(|A|\), so every bias and bound carries over by multiplying through by the constant \(|A|\) (e.g. \(B_{\mathrm{total}} = |A|\,B\), and each cap scales identically).
Argo floats observe \(Y\) at their actual space-time locations \(x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathcal{X}\) (writing \(x_i = (s_i,t_i)\) for the \(i\)-th profile) – point measurements of the true field at arbitrary positions on the sphere and in time, not values on any grid (each profile yields OHC at the float’s lat/lon and date; we treat these as noise-free samples of \(Y\), deferring error from drift during the profile cycle). The locations form a spatio-temporal point pattern whose intensity may be preferential – correlated with \(Y\) through the advecting current. Since \(Y\) is unobserved away from the profiles, \(\mu\) must be estimated; write \(\hat\mu\) for an estimator built from \(\{Y(x_i)\}\) – the Gaussian-process space-time average of nac_gp.qmd, or a simple average of the observations – and \(B = \hat\mu - \mu\) for its bias, the quantity we bound.
What is observable, and what is not. The only observables are the realized profile locations \(\{x_i\}\) and the OHC at those locations \(\{Y(x_i)\}\). The estimand \(\mu\), the field \(Y\) away from the profiles, and the field summaries \(\sigma_Y\), \(\sigma_w\), \(\rho_{w,Y}\) that enter the caps below are not directly observable. This distinction is load-bearing and we keep it explicit throughout. The theoretical identities and inequalities – the covariance identity of Preferential Sampling Bounds, Cauchy–Schwarz, the RKHS minimax bound of Prediction Bias Bounds – hold for the true field and require no knowledge of \(Y\) off-track; but evaluating any of them, computing the permutation statistic, or verifying a bound requires a bridge to the unobserved field: either a field estimator (the GP, yielding \(\hat Y\), \(\hat\sigma_Y\), \(\hat w\)) or a complete-coverage proxy. For the verification step alone we take the GLORYS12 reanalysis – a data-assimilating model with complete spatiotemporal coverage – as a proxy for \(Y\) (hence for \(\mu\)): an explicit, scoped assumption, examined in Validation and invoked there and nowhere in the derivations.


Bias decomposition
Floats do not sample the domain evenly. Condition throughout on the realized sampling configuration, define the sampling weight function \(w(x) \ge 0\): \(w > 1\) where the design concentrates and \(w < 1\) where it is sparse, normalized so its space-time average is one, \(\mathbb{E}[w] = 1\) (all such moments are with respect to \(\nu\), per Section 1). This \(w\) is the deterministic weight of the conditioned design (formally a Radon–Nikodym derivative – see the callout); it is distinct both from the random, atomic empirical point pattern itself and from any estimate \(\hat w\) built from that pattern, and we keep the three separate. An evenly spread, representative design has \(w \equiv 1\); sampling is preferential when \(w\) is correlated with the field \(Y\). For example, if the unnormalized \(w > 1\) where Y is high the bias is positive. The canonical model makes the weight log-linear in the field, \(w(x) \propto
\exp\{\beta\,Y(x)\}\), with \(\beta \ne 0\) the degree of preferentiality (Diggle et al. 2010). (The callout below gives the measure-theoretic definition of \(w\) as a point-process intensity, and the assumptions it requires.)
Naively averaging the float observations does not recover the domain mean \(\mu = \mathbb{E}[Y]\) but the design-mean – the field averaged with the floats’ own weighting –
\[\begin{gather*}
\mu_w \;=\; \mathbb{E}[w\,Y] .
\end{gather*}\]
Inserting \(\mu_w\) between the estimate and the estimand gives an exact two-term decomposition on the continuous domain of Section 1 – no partition of \(\mathcal{X}\) is introduced, so the identity is grid-free (though every practical evaluation of its pieces uses quadrature or Monte-Carlo integration; see Validation):
\[\begin{gather*}
B \;=\; \hat\mu - \mu \;=\; \underbrace{\big(\mu_w - \mu\big)}_{\displaystyle B_{\mathrm{samp}}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\big(\hat\mu - \mu_w\big)}_{\displaystyle B_{\mathrm{pred}}} .
\end{gather*}\]
The split is an algebraic identity, exact for every realization and for any \(w\) with \(\mathbb{E}[w] = 1\).

\(B_{\mathrm{samp}}\) – the preferential-sampling bias (we use this term rather than the broader survey-sampling label “design bias”, with which it coincides here) depends only on where and when the floats sample (through \(w\)) and the field, not on how we reconstruct it: the gap between the sampling-weighted mean and the domain mean. It is the pure “floats went to the warm/cold water” effect; Preferential Sampling Bounds shows \(B_{\mathrm{samp}} = \operatorname{Cov}(w, Y)\).
\(B_{\mathrm{pred}}\) – the prediction bias is the estimator’s departure from the design-mean \(\mu_w\): the error incurred in turning sparse point observations into an areal field estimate. Its mechanism is a change of support – reconciling the point-support observations with the block/areal quantity we actually want (the standard geostatistical name; Gelfand et al. (2001)), the same effect called representativeness error in data assimilation (Janjić et al. 2018).

What is observable is the sample mean of OHC at the profiles, \(\bar Y = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i
Y(x_i)\). The realized preferential-sampling bias is \(\bar Y - \mu\); because it subtracts the unknown \(\mu\), it is a target to be verified, not a quantity computable from the data alone – it can only be evaluated against the proxy field (Validation), and we therefore do not call it an estimator. Two points guard against over-claiming here. First, \(\bar Y\) does not converge to \(\mu\): under a preferential design \(\bar Y \to \mu_w\) (the design-mean), so \(\bar Y - \mu \to \mu_w -
\mu = B_{\mathrm{samp}}\) – and even this limit requires point-process regularity (e.g. ergodicity / increasing-domain asymptotics) that we state rather than assume silently. Second, the two components admit different handles: \(B_{\mathrm{samp}}\) is a design quantity (a covariance between the sampling weight and the field), bounded distribution-free (Preferential Sampling Bounds); \(B_{\mathrm{pred}}\) is an approximation-theory quantity (a linear-functional reconstruction error over a smoothness class), bounded through the interpolator’s RKHS structure (Prediction Bias Bounds). For the interpolate-everywhere GP estimator the RKHS machinery bounds the total error \(\hat\mu - \mu\) directly against the continuous estimand – the target functional is \(L(Y) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y\,d\nu\) – under the smoothness assumptions catalogued in Prediction Bias Bounds; the design split is what additionally isolates and caps the preferential-sampling contribution on its own.






NoteMeasure-theoretic formulation of the sampling design





The floats realize a spatio-temporal point process \(N\) on \((\mathcal{X},\, \mathcal{B}(A)\otimes\mathcal{B}(\mathcal{T}))\) with intensity measure \(\Lambda(E) = \mathbb{E}_N[N(E)]\) (the expected number of profiles falling in \(E\)). Assume \(\Lambda \ll \nu\) (the sampling has no singular part relative to space-time volume); the sampling weight is then the Radon–Nikodym derivative
\[\begin{gather*}
w \;=\; \frac{d\pi}{d\nu} \in L^2(\nu), \qquad \pi \;=\; \frac{\Lambda}{\Lambda(\mathcal{X})},
\qquad \int w \,d\nu \;=\; \mathbb{E}[w] \;=\; 1 ,
\end{gather*}\]
of the normalized sampling measure \(\pi\) with respect to \(\nu\), and non-preferential sampling is \(\pi = \nu\). The log-linear model of Diggle et al. (2010) is \(d\Lambda/d\nu \propto \exp\{\beta\,Y\}\), and the design-mean is the field integrated against the sampling measure, \(\mu_w = \int Y\,d\pi = \int w\,Y\,d\nu = \mathbb{E}[w\,Y]\).
We assume \(w \in L^2(\nu)\) – this is what makes \(\sigma_w\) finite and Cauchy–Schwarz applicable, but it is a genuine restriction: highly concentrated designs (in the limit, the purely atomic empirical pattern of a finite sample, which is not absolutely continuous w.r.t. \(\nu\)) admit no such density, and the bounds below then apply to a regularized/estimated intensity \(\hat w \in L^2\) rather than to the raw point pattern.
Two probabilistic caveats. Campbell’s theorem gives \(\mathbb{E}_N\big[\sum_i Y(x_i)\big] = \int Y\,d\Lambda\) – an identity in expectation, nothing more. It does not by itself imply that the empirical measure \(\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \delta_{x_i}\) converges to \(\pi\), nor that \(\bar Y \to \mu_w\); that convergence is a law of large numbers for the point process, requiring the ergodicity / increasing-domain conditions noted in the main text. We therefore treat \(B_{\mathrm{samp}} = \mu_w - \mu\) as defined by the conditioned design – the regime in which the covariance identity of Preferential Sampling Bounds is exact – and read \(\bar Y - \mu\) as its proxy-evaluated realization, not as a consistent estimator handed over for free by Campbell’s theorem.





References


Diggle, Peter J., Raquel Menezes, and Ting-li Su. 2010. “Geostatistical Inference Under Preferential Sampling.” Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series C (Applied Statistics) 59 (2): 191–232.


Gelfand, Alan E., Li Zhu, and Bradley P. Carlin. 2001. “On the Change of Support Problem for Spatio-Temporal Data.” Biostatistics 2 (1): 31–45.


Janjić, Tijana, Niels Bormann, Marc Bocquet, et al. 2018. “On the Representation Error in Data Assimilation.” Quarterly Journal of the Royal Meteorological Society 144 (713): 1257–78.

title: Permutation Test
text:
The permutation construction below produces a reference (null) distribution for the preferential-sampling bias under the hypothesis of no temperature preference, which we could use to interpret the realized bias and to gauge how conservative the deterministic caps are, never as a tighter bound.
Consider the null hypothesis “the floats show no preference for warm/cold water,” i.e. the sampling intensity does not co-vary with \(Y\).
Constrained-null resampling. Let \(\lambda_0\) be an estimated non-preferential intensity on \(\mathcal{X}\); in practice a smooth intensity fit to the realized pattern with the field regressed out, or a mechanistic deployment-and-advection intensity, normalized to the probability measure \(\pi_0 \propto \lambda_0\). For \(b = 1,\dots,B\) we draw a null configuration \(\{x_i^{(b)}\}_{i=1}^{n}\) (each \(x_i^{(b)} = (s_i^{(b)}, t_i^{(b)})\)) as an i.i.d. sample from \(\pi_0\) and recompute the realized preferential-sampling statistic against the field held fixed:
\[\begin{gather*}
B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i Y\big(x_i^{(b)}\big) - \mu , \qquad b = 1,\dots,B .
\end{gather*}\]
Observability. Both \(B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}\) and the realized statistic \(\bar Y - \mu\) depend on \(\mu\) and on \(Y\) away from the profiles, so – exactly as in Bias decomposition – the permutation test is computed against the proxy field (or a GP estimate \(\hat Y\)), not from float observations alone. It is a validation-stage diagnostic, not a field-free guarantee.
What it delivers.

Evidence for preferential sampling – a Monte-Carlo \(p\)-value locating the realized bias in the null,

\[\begin{gather*}
p \;=\; \frac{1 + \#\{\, b : |B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}| \ge |\bar Y - \mu| \,\}}{B + 1}.
\end{gather*}\]
A realized bias in the tail is evidence that floats sample \(Y\) preferentially, over and above the physical constraints encoded in \(\lambda_0\). 2. A conservativeness diagnostic – the spread of \(\{B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}\}\) is the bias the sampling geometry alone would produce with no temperature preference; the gap between an upper null quantile \(q_{0.975}\big(\{\,|B_{\mathrm{samp}}^{(b)}|\,\}\big)\) and the Cauchy–Schwarz cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) measures how much slack the worst-case bound carries. That quantile is a property of the null, not an upper bound on the realized \(B_{\mathrm{samp}}\).

title: Validation
text:
Reanalysis
The bounds above are properties of the true field \(Y\) and the realized float locations; the inequalities never assume \(Y\) is known between profiles. To check them, however, we need the realized bias – hence the full field and \(\mu\) – so this is the one section where the observability bridge of Motivation and estimand is cashed in: we take the GLORYS12 reanalysis as a proxy for \(Y\) (hence \(\mu\)), an explicit, scoped assumption invoked here and nowhere in the derivations. With the proxy field in hand we form the realized quantities over the space-time domain: the total bias \(B = \hat\mu - \mu\) (GP space-time mean of nac_gp.qmd minus the proxy domain mean), and the realized preferential-sampling bias \(\bar Y - \mu = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i Y(x_i) - \mu\) (the proxy field at the profiles minus its domain mean). Neither is observable without the proxy – which is exactly why they live here. (The exploratory, grid-based per-month ohc_bias.compute_bias is a diagnostic, not the rigorous decomposition.)
The identities are grid-free; the evaluations are not. Every realized quantity above, and every ingredient of the caps – \(\mu\), \(\sigma_Y\), \(\sigma_w\), the representer norm \(\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\), and the permutation statistics – is computed numerically: by quadrature on the GLORYS grid, on the GP prediction grid, or by Monte-Carlo integration, each carrying its own (bounded or estimated) error. The mathematics of Bias decomposition through Prediction Bias Bounds is grid-free; the implementation is not. Two points enter here and only here:

Grid quadrature. GLORYS supplies values on its \(1/12^\circ\) grid over the resolved times, so the domain mean is a space-time quadrature \(\mu \approx \mu_N = \sum_c \nu(c)\, Y_c\) over space-time cells \(c\) (\(\sum_c \nu(c) = 1\)) – a numerical evaluation of the estimand’s defining integral \(\mu = \int_{\mathcal{X}} Y\,d\nu\) of Motivation and estimand, in the spirit of the area-measure quadrature of Baugh and McKinnon (2022). (The per-month ohc.truth_domain_mean is the exploratory, spatial-only version; the rigorous estimand aggregates over \(\mathcal{X}\).) The resulting quadrature error \(B_{\mathrm{quad}} = \mu_N - \mu\) is a deterministic bounded linear functional of \(Y\), capped by the RKHS machinery of Prediction Bias Bounds (\(|B_{\mathrm{quad}}| \le R\|q\|_{\mathcal H}\), \(q = \sum_c \nu(c)\, k(\cdot,x_c) - \xi_\nu\)) and negligible at \(1/12^\circ\).
Proxy fidelity. The realized bias is computed against the reanalysis, not the ocean; validation certifies the bounding machinery, with reanalysis error a separate, acknowledged layer (cross- reanalysis spread is deferred).

We then report, per depth horizon:

Coverage – that each deterministic cap dominates the realized quantity it bounds. The Cauchy–Schwarz cap holds for the design-defined \(B_{\mathrm{samp}}\) exactly (given the design); its proxy realization \(\bar Y - \mu\) is dominated up to the point-process sampling variability of Bias decomposition, and the RKHS / posterior-SE cap should dominate the realized total bias.
Tightness – the ratio of bound to realized bias, quantifying the slack each guarantee carries.
Calibration – the permutation \(p\)-values, and the GP interval coverage rates against the proxy (the honesty check for the probabilistic bound of Prediction Bias Bounds).

A bound that is provably valid and demonstrably covers the directly-computed proxy bias is what makes the result credible rather than merely formal – subject to the reanalysis-proxy caveat above.


References


Baugh, Samuel, and Karen McKinnon. 2022. “Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of Ocean Heat Content and Its Uncertainty.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2110.09717.

title: Targeted Analysis
text:
Goal and design
TODO


Setup
This report targets the same Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current analysis region as pilot_simulation.qmd, but compares the GLORYS truth OHC field against the RG Argo Climatology across three years (2020, 2021, 2022) rather than just one. 2020 velocity and temperature come from data/velocity_gs_wide/ (lat 30–48\(^\circ\)N); 2021 and 2022 come from data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/ (lat 33–45\(^\circ\)N, downloaded by code/download_gs_2021_2022.py), a tighter latitude band over the same longitude span and GLORYS12V1 product. Both cover the analysis region with room to spare.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim
from trajplots import map_fields_row

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

# --- per-year velocity/temperature source and its download domain ---
VELOCITY_GLOB = {
    2020: "../data/velocity_gs_wide/velocity_2020_*.nc",
    2021: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2021_*.nc",
    2022: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2022_*.nc",
}
DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS = {  # (lat0, lat1, lon0, lon1) -- as downloaded, differs by year
    2020: (30, 48, -74, -49),
    2021: (33, 45, -74, -49),
    2022: (33, 45, -74, -49),
}

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)     # float deployment box (4$^\circ$ lat x 6$^\circ$ lon)
CONTEXT_BOUNDS  = (34, 42, -70, -60)     # ANALYSIS_BOUNDS + 2 deg margin, inside every year's domain
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]     # cartopy map extent for the wide-context maps
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]   # both depth layers, shown throughout




Analysis region
The analysis domain sits on the Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current front — a sharp north–south OHC gradient. The annual-mean GLORYS OHC below shows high OHC (~90 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the south and low OHC (~45 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the north, for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
OHC definition. For every 1/12\(^\circ\) grid cell, OHC (GJ m\(^{-2}\)) is the integral \(\text{OHC} = \int_0^{z} \rho\, c_p\, T\, dz\) computed using the trapezoidal rule for \(z = 700\) m and \(z = 2000\) m where \(T\) is the sea water potential temperature in \(^\circ\)C reported at 50 standard depth levels (41 levels from 0m-2250m: 0.41m, 1.54m, …, 644m, 763m, …, 1942m, 2225m). Unlike Levitus et al. [2012], which computed \(\rho\) and \(c_p\) from annual climatological T/S fields, here both are fixed constants – adequate at this study’s scope (a \(4^\circ\times6^\circ\) region and a bias between two estimators computed the same way, so most of the T/S-dependence common to both cancels) but worth revisiting if the analysis later targets absolute OHC magnitudes or multi-year trends, where Levitus-style climatological \(\rho,c_p\) would matter more.


Load per-year GLORYS velocity/temperature and compute the true OHC field
# Cache truth_ohc_field (reads all 41 depth levels -- the dominant cost here)
# to NetCDF so re-rendering doesn't repeat it. Delete the .nc to force a recompute.
OHC_TRUTH_DIR = "../data/ohc_truth"
os.makedirs(OHC_TRUTH_DIR, exist_ok=True)

theta = {}
truth_field_full = {}
for y in YEARS:
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS[y]
    theta[y] = xr.open_mfdataset(VELOCITY_GLOB[y]).sel(
        latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1),
    )
    cache_path = f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_ohc_{y}.nc"
    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        truth_field_full[y] = xr.open_dataset(cache_path).load()
    else:
        truth_field_full[y] = ohc.truth_ohc_field(theta[y]).compute()
        truth_field_full[y].to_netcdf(cache_path)

_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2020)

Figure 6: GLORYS 2022-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.










OHC Anomaly
Climatology source. data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_Temperature_2019.nc (the RG Argo Climatology) contains ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN: a single static mean temperature field (Jan 2004 – Dec 2018, no seasonal/monthly dimension) on a 1\(^\circ\) lat/lon grid and 58 pressure levels (2.5–1975 dbar). Its longitude is 0–360\(^\circ\)E and is converted to -180..180\(^\circ\) on load.
The twelve monthly extension files RG_ArgoClim_<YYYY><MM>_2019.nc each contain the observed ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY from the 2004–2018 mean for each calendar month. We load these for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
Climatological OHC field. Either mean-temperature field is integrated over depth with the same trapezoidal rule used for GLORYS (ohc.profile_ohc, treating RG’s pressure in dbar as meters, the same approximation already used throughout this report), giving an ohc_700/ohc_2000 field on RG’s native 1\(^\circ\) grid.
Linear interpolation from 1\(^\circ\) to 1/12\(^\circ\). Because the true GLORYS temperature field is on a 1/12\(^\circ\) grid, code/ohc_climatology.py bilinearly interpolates the climatological OHC to the 1/12\(^\circ\) grid for each calendar month.
Anomaly. For a grid cell at \(x = (x_{lat}, x_{lon})\) observed in month \(t\) of year \(y\), the OHC anomaly is calculated by subtracting the spatially interpolated climatological OHC for that calendar month (built from that year’s RG extension file) from the observed monthly-mean GLORYS OHC value. This is repeated independently for 2020, 2021, and 2022, each against its own year’s RG climatology anomaly.


Load the RG climatology (seasonal, per year + static) and compute the truth OHC anomaly for 2020-2022
mean_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_mean()
clim_field_B = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da)  # static 2004-2018 mean

clim_field = {}       # per-year seasonal climatology (Option A), month dim 1-12
truth_field = {}      # per-year truth field restricted to the analysis region
truth_anom = {}       # per-year GLORYS truth OHC anomaly (native 1/12$^\circ$)
truth_anom_cells = {}
for y in YEARS:
    ext_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_extensions(year=y)
    clim_field[y] = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da + ext_da).compute()
    truth_field[y] = truth_field_full[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3]),
    )
    truth_anom[y] = ohc_clim.truth_ohc_anomaly(truth_field[y], clim_field[y])
    truth_anom_cells[y] = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_anom[y])

# truth_field[y]'s spatial grid is identical across years (same ANALYSIS_BOUNDS
# slice of the same native 1/12 deg grid) -- only the time axis differs -- so
# concatenating along time lets downstream monthly-mean/coarsen calls run once
# on all three years instead of once per year.
truth_field_all = xr.concat([truth_field[y] for y in YEARS], dim="time")

# All three years concatenated -- one 36-month table for the multi-year maps/limits below.
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.concat(truth_anom_cells.values(), ignore_index=True)

# Publish both to data/ so nac_gp.qmd (and any other downstream report) can load
# them directly instead of re-deriving them from the raw RG climatology files.
truth_field_all.to_netcdf(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc")
truth_anom_cells_all.to_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", index=False)

# Shared diverging colour range for every *anomaly* map below, across all years.
ANOM_VLIM = {d: float(truth_anom_cells_all[d].abs().quantile(0.98)) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ for d in DEPTHS}


Below is comparison of the RG climatology published temperature trend and my recreation



Published RG Climatology global-mean temperature time series (source: sio-argo.ucsd.edu)




Reconstruct published RG global-mean temperature time series (2004–2026)
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from pathlib import Path

_ds_rg  = xr.open_dataset(ohc_clim.RG_MEAN_PATH, decode_times=False)
_mean_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN"].values    # (pres, lat, lon)
_anom_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values # (180, pres, lat, lon)
_p      = _ds_rg["PRESSURE"].values
_lat    = _ds_rg["LATITUDE"].values
_ds_rg.close()

_dp     = np.diff(_p)
_coslat = np.cos(np.deg2rad(_lat))

def _vol_mean_T(T):
    T_mid = (T[:-1] + T[1:]) / 2    # trapezoidal averages; NaN where either endpoint NaN
    w     = _dp[:, None, None] * _coslat[None, :, None]
    return float(np.nansum(T_mid * w) / np.nansum(np.where(np.isnan(T_mid), 0.0, w)))

_dates_main = pd.date_range("2004-01", periods=180, freq="MS")
_tmean_main = np.array([_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom_v[i]) for i in range(180)])

# Load all available extension months (2019 onward)
_ext_targets = [(y, m) for y in range(2019, 2027) for m in range(1, 13)
                if not (y == 2026 and m > 2)]
_ext_dates, _ext_vals = [], []
for _y, _m in _ext_targets:
    _path = f"../data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_{_y}{_m:02d}_2019.nc"
    if not Path(_path).exists():
        continue
    _ds = xr.open_dataset(_path, decode_times=False)
    _anom = _ds["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values.squeeze(0)
    _ext_vals.append(_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom))
    _ext_dates.append(pd.Timestamp(_y, _m, 1))
    _ds.close()

# Bridge the Dec 2018 / Jan 2019 gap by prepending the last climatology point
# to the extension series so the two line segments share an endpoint.
_bridge_date = pd.Timestamp(2018, 12, 1)
_bridge_val  = _tmean_main[-1]

_all_dates = np.array([_bridge_date] + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_all_vals  = np.concatenate([[_bridge_val], _ext_vals])

_smooth  = pd.Series(
    np.concatenate([_tmean_main, _ext_vals]),
).rolling(12, center=True, min_periods=12).mean().values
_sm_dates = np.array(list(_dates_main) + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_sm_ok    = ~np.isnan(_smooth)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(_dates_main, _tmean_main, color="black", lw=0.8, label="2004–2018 (mean + anom)")
ax.plot(_all_dates, _all_vals, color="red", lw=0.8,
        label=f"2019–{_ext_dates[-1].year} extensions")
ax.plot(_sm_dates[_sm_ok], _smooth[_sm_ok], color="limegreen", lw=1,
        label="12-month running mean")
for y in YEARS:
    ax.axvspan(pd.Timestamp(y, 1, 1), pd.Timestamp(y, 12, 31), color="#4a90d9", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_ylabel("Potential Temperature ($^\circ$C)")
ax.set_xlim(_dates_main[0], _all_dates[-1])
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(2))
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y"))
ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
fig.tight_layout()








Figure 7: Verification: global-mean RG potential temperature reconstructed from mean + monthly extension anomalies. The ~0.04 \(^\circ\)C upward offset vs the published plot above is expected — the RG website excludes marginal seas and the Arctic from its spatial average; we include all non-NaN cells.







RG climatology (static), native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\)

RG climatology by year and month, native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\), the GLORYS truth, the GLORYS truth - RG climatology anomaly, and eastward/northward velocity
# Fixed view for every panel below, regardless of its native grid resolution.
ANALYSIS_XLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3])
ANALYSIS_YLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1])

_clim_A_plot_years, _clim_A_interp_cells_years = [], []
for y in YEARS:
    months_y = [pd.Timestamp(y, m, 1) for m in clim_field[y]["month"].values]
    plot_y = clim_field[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[0], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[2], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[3]),
    ).assign_coords(month=months_y)
    _clim_A_plot_years.append(plot_y)
    _clim_A_interp_cells_years.append(
        ohc_clim.climatology_cells_table(
            clim_field[y], truth_field_full[2020]["latitude"].values, truth_field_full[2020]["longitude"].values,
            months=months_y,
        )
    )

# One 36-month table per map kind, across all three years.
_clim_A_native_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(xr.concat(_clim_A_plot_years, dim="month"))
_clim_A_interp_cells = pd.concat(_clim_A_interp_cells_years, ignore_index=True)

# GLORYS truth, monthly-mean, same grid as the anomaly. truth_field_all already
# carries real per-year dates, so one resample over all three years gives
# correctly-dated months directly -- no per-year loop/relabeling needed.
truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
truth_monthly_cells_all = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)

# Velocity near the 1000 m parking depth, monthly-mean, analysis region only.
# theta[y] itself can't be concatenated (different years cover different lat/lon
# extents -- see DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS), but restricting each to ANALYSIS_BOUNDS first
# gives identical grids that can be, so the resample below still runs once.
_theta_analysis_all = xr.concat(
    [
        theta[y][["uo", "vo"]].sel(depth=1000, method="nearest").sel(
            latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),

    ],

)

def show_clim_map(kind, d):

    if kind == "truth":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            truth_monthly_cells_all, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,

    if kind == "anom":

def show_velocity_map(component):

def _box_mean(field, bounds=ANALYSIS_BOUNDS, value_cols=DEPTHS):
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = bounds
    sub = field.sel(latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1))
    w = np.cos(np.deg2rad(sub["latitude"]))
    return sub[list(value_cols)].weighted(w).mean(dim=("latitude", "longitude"))

box_by_year = {y: _box_mean(clim_field[y]) for y in YEARS}  # dims: month (1-12), per year
box_B = _box_mean(clim_field_B)  # scalar, static 2004-2018 reference

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4), sharex=True)
months = np.arange(1, 13)
palette = plt.get_cmap("tab10").colors
for ax, d in zip(axes, DEPTHS):

        ax.plot(months, box_by_year[y][d] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "-o", color=palette[i], label=f"{y} mean")
    ax.axhline(float(box_B[d]) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, color="0.4", ls="--", label="static 2004-2018")
    ax.set_title(f"{d} background OHC, analysis-region mean")
    ax.set_xlabel("calendar month")
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
axes[0].set_ylabel("OHC (GJ m$^{-2}$)")
axes[0].legend(frameon=False, fontsize="small")
